title: EKG Interpretation: A Systematic Approach
objectives: Apply a systematic approach to 12-lead EKG interpretation
Differentiate STEMI patterns by coronary territory
Recognize common EKG mimics and pitfalls

sections.title: Systematic EKG Approach
sections.content:
**Every EKG should be read systematically:**

1. **Rate**: 300/RR interval (large boxes) or 10-second strip × 6
2. **Rhythm**: Regular? P waves? P-QRS relationship?
3. **Axis**: Normal (-30° to +90°), LAD, RAD
4. **Intervals**: PR (120-200ms), QRS (<120ms), QTc (<440ms men, <460ms women)
5. **ST segments/T waves**: Elevation, depression, inversions
6. **Other**: Hypertrophy, bundle branch blocks, Q waves

sections.title: Life-Threatening Patterns
sections.content:
**Recognize immediately:**

• **STEMI**: ST elevation ≥1mm in 2+ contiguous leads (≥2mm in V1-V3)
• **Hyperkalemia**: Peaked T waves → widened QRS → sine wave → asystole
• **Complete heart block**: P waves march through, slow ventricular rate
• **Ventricular tachycardia**: Wide complex tachycardia, AV dissociation
• **Brugada pattern**: Coved ST elevation V1-V3
• **Long QT**: QTc >500ms → risk of Torsades de Pointes
• **Massive PE**: S1Q3T3, right heart strain, new RBBB

sections.title: STEMI Localization
sections.content:
**Coronary territory mapping:**

• **Anterior/LAD**: V1-V4 (septal V1-V2, anterior V3-V4)
• **Lateral/LCx**: I, aVL, V5-V6
• **Inferior/RCA**: II, III, aVF
• **Posterior**: Reciprocal depression V1-V3, tall R waves V1-V2
• **Right ventricle**: V4R elevation (get right-sided leads in inferior STEMI)

**STEMI equivalents:**
• De Winter T waves (upsloping ST depression + peaked T in precordials)
• Wellens syndrome (deep T inversions V1-V4 = critical LAD stenosis)
• New LBBB with concordant ST changes (Sgarbossa criteria)

sections.title: Common Pitfalls
sections.content:
**EKG mimics to know:**

• Early repolarization: Diffuse ST elevation, concave up, young patient
• LVH with strain: ST depression/T inversion in lateral leads
• Pericarditis: Diffuse ST elevation + PR depression
• Benign T wave inversion: Persistent juvenile pattern
• Digitalis effect: Scooped ST depression ('Salvador Dali mustache')

**Artifacts:**
• Limb lead reversal: Inverted P and QRS in lead I
• Tremor artifact: Irregular baseline mimicking AFib
• Poor R wave progression: Lead placement vs pathology

mcqs.stem: An ECG shows ST elevation in leads II, III, and aVF with reciprocal ST depression in I and aVL. Which coronary artery is most likely occluded?

options.explanation: ST elevation in inferior leads (II, III, aVF) with reciprocal changes in lateral leads (I, aVL) localizes to an inferior STEMI, most commonly caused by RCA occlusion (~85% of cases). Check right-sided leads (V4R) to assess for RV involvement.
LCx causes lateral STEMI (I, aVL, V5-V6). While LCx can cause inferior changes in ~15% of cases (dominant LCx), RCA is far more common.
LAD occlusion causes anterior STEMI with ST elevation in V1-V4 (± V5-V6), not inferior leads.
Left main occlusion causes widespread ST depression with ST elevation in aVR — a pattern of diffuse subendocardial ischemia, not isolated inferior STEMI.

correct.deeperWhy: Understanding this concept is foundational for clinical reasoning in this domain.

coachDx.misconception: Left main occlusion causes widespread ST depression with ST elevation in aVR — a pattern of diffuse subendocardial ischemia, not isolated inferior STEMI.
coachDx.guidance: Review the key features that distinguish Right coronary artery (RCA) from Left main coronary artery.
coachDx.bridgeTo: The key distinction involves Right coronary artery (RCA).
